title: Invisible Code Hits GitHub
summary: A supply-chain attack using invisible Unicode code has affected GitHub and other repositories. The attack exploits code that is invisible to the human eye.
body:
A recent supply-chain attack has hit GitHub and other repositories, utilizing invisible Unicode code to exploit vulnerabilities. This type of code is invisible to the human eye, making it difficult to detect. 

 The attack has significant implications for the security of open-source software, as it can be used to inject malicious code into repositories without being noticed. The use of invisible Unicode code is a relatively new tactic, and it has caught the attention of attackers. 

 The incident has raised concerns about the security of online repositories and the need for more robust detection methods. As the attack is still being investigated, it is unclear what the full extent of the damage is, but it is likely to have a significant impact on the industry. Reactions from developers and repository owners are pending, and it is expected that new measures will be taken to prevent similar attacks in the future.
twitterThread: 🚨 Breaking: Invisible code hits GitHub! 🚨 Attackers are using Unicode that's invisible to the human eye to exploit vulnerabilities.
Here's why this matters: The attack can inject malicious code into repositories without being noticed. Let that sink in.
The wild part: This type of attack is relatively new, and it's caught the attention of attackers. GitHub and other repositories are affected.
This changes everything for open-source software security. We need more robust detection methods to prevent similar attacks.
The future of online repositories is at risk. What's next? Will we see a new wave of attacks using invisible code?
